coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient carbon quantum dot suspensions and membranes for sensitive/selective detection and adsorption/recovery of mercury ions from aqueous solutions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Chun-Chieh
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Juang, Ruey-Shin
Yang, Jou-Wen
Gu, Siyong
Gandomi, Yasser Ashraf
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs), synthesized through a solid-phase microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 250 °C, were employed as highly-selective/-sensitive probe as well as an efficient adsorbent toward Hg2+ ions that exist in aqueous solutions. The spherical CQDs possess a homogeneous dispersion with a narrow distribution, ranged from 2.5 to 5.5 nm. The fluorescence responses from CQD samples demonstrate triple band at ca. 450, 500 and 520 nm, influenced by the ratio of citric acid to urea in the carbon precursor. The fluorescence quenching ratio was found to be proportional to the Hg2+ concentration within the solution. Such a correlation was formulated using Stern–Volmer model. The detection limit of CQDs toward Hg2+ ions reached as high as 10 ppb, and the maximal adsorption capacity of Hg2+ onto CQDs was as high as 3.33 g/g, which is 1280, 6, and 16 times higher than the values reported for the active carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes, respectively. The CQD-coated polymeric membrane also exhibits the fluorescence quenching and it is easily regenerated by iodide anions for subsequent usages. Accordingly, the functionalized CQDs pave the pathway for engineering the adsorption and recovery of toxic Hg2+ contaminant that are present in aqueous solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-phase microwave-assisted method
Carbon quantum dots
Adsorption capacity
Fluorescence quenching
Mercury ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs by SPMA method
Physiochemical and optical properties of CQDs
Fluorescence quenching and adsorption of Hg2+ on CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very grateful for the support from the Ministry of Science and Technology (Taiwan, R.O.C.) under the contracts MOST 105-2628-E-155-002-MY3 and MOST 105-2221-E-155-014-MY3. Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2E0082 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou , Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation , Taiwan) is deeply appreciated.

The authors are very grateful for the support from the Ministry of Science and Technology (Taiwan, R.O.C.) under the contracts MOST 105-2628-E-155-002-MY3 and MOST 105-2221-E-155-014-MY3. Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2E0082 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation, Taiwan) is deeply appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM micrographs of (a) CQD-1, (b) CQD-2, and (c) CQD-3 samples. The inset of (a)–(c) shows their selected-area diffraction rings on CQD-1, CQD-2, and CQD-3 samples, respectively. High-resolution lattice fringes of (d) CQD-1, (e) CQD-2, and (f) CQD-3 samples.
XPS O 1s peaks of (a) CQD-1, (b) CQD-2, and (c) CQD-3 samples, and XPS N 1s peaks of (d) CQD-1, (e) CQD-2, and (f) CQD-3 samples, deconvoluted by a multiple Gaussian function.

(a) Photographs of different CQD suspensions without and with 50 ppm Hg2+ ions under UV irradiation. PL emission spectra of (a) CQD-1, (b) CQD-2, and (c) CQD-3 samples after adding different concentrations of Hg2+ ions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Typical Stern–Volmer plots of different CQDs toward Hg2+ ions within the concentration range of (a) 0‒5000 ppb (for all GQDs), (b) 10‒100 ppb (for CQD-1), (c) 1000‒5000 ppb (for GQD-1), and (d) 500‒5000 ppb (for GQD-2 and GQD-3).
(a) Photograph of sediment of Hg species on the bottom of CQD suspensions. FE-SEM images of the sediments on CQD-1 suspension at (b) low and (c) high magnifications.

The schematic diagram for the fluorescence quenching and adsorption of mercury ions on functionalized CQDs, consisted of (a) fluorescence emitting under UV irradiation, (b) fluorescence quenching due to the formation of CQD‒(Hg2+) x complex, and (c) sedimentation of Hg-based spheres when reaching adsorption saturation. The inset of Fig. 6 shows three possible sites for adsorbing Hg species including (i) carboxylic, (ii) amide-carbonyl, and (iii) phenolic groups.

Photograph of different CQD-coated PVdF membranes under (a) visible light illumination and under UV irradiation (b) without Hg2+ ions, (c) with 10 ppm Hg2+ ions, and (d) with 10 ppm iodide anions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Adsorption isotherm of Hg2+ ions onto CQD-1 adsorbent at 30 °C, where the solid symbol is experimental data, and solid curves are Freundlich and Langmuir models.
Comparison among the detection limits toward Hg2+ ions by different CQD samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Remark: NCQD: N-doped CQD; NS-CQD: N-/S-doped CQD; DNA-Au-NP: Deoxyribonucleic acid-modified Au nanoparticles; Au-NPs: Au nanoparticles; SOCQD: S-/O-doped CQD; Mn-ZnSe/ZnS: Mn-doped ZnSe/ZnS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The most suitable parameters of Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms for adsorption of mercury ions onto CQDs at 30 °C.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient carbon quantum dot suspensions and membranes for sensitive/selective detection and adsorption/recovery of mercury ions from aqueous solutions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs), synthesized through a solid-phase microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 250 °C, were employed as highly-selective/-sensitive probe as well as an efficient adsorbent toward Hg2+ ions that exist in aqueous solutions. The spherical CQDs possess a homogeneous dispersion with a narrow distribution, ranged from 2.5 to 5.5 nm. The fluorescence responses from CQD samples demonstrate triple band at ca. 450, 500 and 520 nm, influenced by the ratio of citric acid to urea in the carbon precursor. The fluorescence quenching ratio was found to be proportional to the Hg2+ concentration within the solution. Such a correlation was formulated using Stern–Volmer model. The detection limit of CQDs toward Hg2+ ions reached as high as 10 ppb, and the maximal adsorption capacity of Hg2+ onto CQDs was as high as 3.33 g/g, which is 1280, 6, and 16 times higher than the values reported for the active carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes, respectively. The CQD-coated polymeric membrane also exhibits the fluorescence quenching and it is easily regenerated by iodide anions for subsequent usages. Accordingly, the functionalized CQDs pave the pathway for engineering the adsorption and recovery of toxic Hg2+ contaminant that are present in aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Materials with wide photo-absorption and -emission characteristics are commonly used for applications involving photo-detection, solar energy absorption, and bio-imaging [1]. Graphene, a hexagonal honeycomb sp2-bonded single- or several-layer carbon material [2–4], belongs to this class of materials. Graphene is capable of exhibiting a broadband absorption (, 300–2500 nm) [4] but has almost no optical emission due to possessing a zero-band gap structure. Therefore, the application of graphene for the optoelectronic devices as well as the photo-detectors is extremely limited. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), however, are emerged as an efficient fluorescent material. GQDs are environmentally friendly, non-toxic, biocompatible, and robust [5]. GQDs are indeed small-scale graphene with an average particle size in the range of 3–20 nm [6].

Two strategies are commonly employed to manipulate the band gap of GQDs: (i) reducing the particle size down to ∼10 nm, thus altering the band structure due to quantum confinement and edge effects [7,8] and (ii) functionalizing the carbon surface (e.g., surface oxidation or amidation level) by adatom decoration or doping and subsequently, tuning the optical band gap, based on an inter-band transition from a higher conduction band to a lower conduction band [1,9–11].

Therefore, electron acceptor/donor doping of GQDs is a promising approach for modulating their optical and electronic properties. Recent efforts in doping graphene with electron-rich nitrogen impart in-plane substitution of N atoms to graphene as n-type semiconductor [12–13]. The incorporation of N atom within the GQD structure facilitates the quantum yields [14] and improves the electro catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction [15,16]. The doping of N in graphene structure is extremely favorable for broad photo-absorption in wide spectral range since it enhances the carrier transport mobility, improves the chemical stability, and results in high fluorescence quantum yield. Therefore, it can be used as a fluorescence probe for the detection of metal ions (e.g., Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+) with an ultralow detection limit (ppm level) [17–19]. The fluorescence (FL) quenching of GQDs is indeed an in-situ technique to detect the metal ions existing in an aqueous media. This technique is relatively easy to be employed and does not have the sophistications associated with some conventional methods (e.g., chromatography/mass spectrometry and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays) [20]. The achievement directs at novel design of GQD-based textures for detection or removal of metal ions in aqueous solution.

Mercury, an undesired byproduct of paper and pulp industries, oil refining, electrical, rubber processing and fertilizer industries [21], has been recognized as the most toxic metal in natural eco-systems [22]. Concretely, mercury is the priority pollutants listed by United States Environmental Protection Agency. The concentration of Hg2+ ions for discharge into inland surface waters and drinking water must be less than 10 and 1 ppb, respectively (tolerance limit) [23]. High concentration of Hg2+ induces impairment of pulmonary function and kidney, chest pain and dyspnea [24,25]. Numerous efforts have been devoted to removing heavy metal ions from water, such as chemical precipitation [26], ion-exchange [27], flocculation [28], electrolysis [29], membrane separation [30], adsorption [31–33] and photocatalysis [34]. Among these techniques, adsorption method is very promising owing to its easy operation, high removal efficiency, and applicability for various contaminates [35–39]. However, the removal capacity of mercury ions on various adsorbents are relatively low, e.g., 2.6 mg/g (oxidized active carbon) [40], 555 mg/g (ligand-functionalized graphene) [41], and 204 mg/g (amino- and thiol-functionalized carbon nanotubes) [42]; thus, significant improvement is needed.

The present work demonstrates a novel technique for synthesizing highly efficient carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for removal of mercury ions. Herein, CQD suspension serves as a photodetector toward Hg2+ ions as well as a high-performance adsorbent for collecting Hg species within aqueous solution. Here, we have adopted a solid-phase microwave assisted (SPMA) pyrolysis of citric acid mixed with urea for synthesizing CQDs. The application of SPMA method results in high production yield, narrow particle size distribution, and well controlled dopant concentration (N-doped CQDs). The zero-dimensional CQDs were also uniformly coated onto filter papers, forming two-dimensional membranes for the removal of Hg2+ ion in separation units. The robust design of CQD-based device paves the way for employing CQDs for pollutant detection and heavy metal recovery. The unique advantages of this technique make it an ideal method for the removal of heavy metals in large and industrial scales.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs by SPMA method
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized using a solid mixture of citric acid + urea as the precursor within a home-made microwave reactor. The SPMA reactor was equipped with a microwave emitter with a maximal power of 6000 W and a rotary base with a maximal speed of 60 rpm. Three types of CQDs were prepared, based on different recipes of citric acid/urea, 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3 in w/w, which were labeled as CQD-1, CQD-2, and CQD-3, respectively. Prior to SPMA reaction, the carbon precursors (total weight: 100 g) were homogeneously mixed in a three-dimensional ball mixer. The carbon precursors were then placed into a 500 mL quartz tube, and 10 mL distilled water was added into the precursor. The precursors were quickly heated to 250 °C under microwave irradiation. Meanwhile, the tubes were rotated (rotational speed 60 rpm) and the SPMA reaction period was set at 5 min. Subsequently, the white precursors turned brown and black CQD powders inside the tubes. The resulting CQDs were ball-milled and then sieved through a metallic screen (# 200 mesh). The solid precipitate was thoroughly washed in an ultrapure water and finally centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical and optical properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The micro-structural morphology of CQD samples was examined using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; FEI Talos F200s). The chemical composition of the samples was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XP spectra were recorded using a Fison VG ESCA210 spectrometer equipped with Mg‒Kα radiation. The XPS N 1s spectra of all samples were deconvoluted by via a non-linear least squares fitting algorithm with a symmetric Gaussian function. An inductively-coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy/mass spectrometry (ICP-OES/MS) was adopted to analyze the chemical compositions of CQD samples. A fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco FP-8200) was used to analyze the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CGD aqueous suspensions (concentration: 100 ppm). The PL measurements were carried out at a fixed wavelength of 360 nm at ambient temperature. Field-emission scanning electron spectroscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi SU-8200) was used to assess the microstructural morphology of Hg sediment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching and adsorption of Hg2+ on CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Different amounts of Hg2+ solutions were added stepwise and stirred with the CQD suspensions. The detection of mercury ions with CQDs was performed at pH = 7, adjusted by a phosphate buffer solution. The resulting CQD solutions were homogeneously stirred by a magnetic bar and then incubated for 1 min at ambient temperature. The PL emission spectra were recorded at 360 nm by a fluorescence spectrometer. The relation of PL intensity (i.e., F / F 0) versus Hg2+ concentration was generalized, where F 0 and F were the PL intensities of CQD suspensions in the absence and presence Hg2+ ions (at different concentration), respectively.

To inspect the selectivity, eight metal ions including Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, and Cr3+ were added into the CQD suspensions. Various concentrations of ionic solutions (10, 50, and 100 ppm) were prepared for conducting the experiments. The selectivity measurement was carried out at three steps: (i) adding 10 ppm Cd2+ followed by 10 ppm Zn2+ and (ii) adding 10 ppm Cu2+ followed by 10 ppm Ni2+ ionic solution, into 1 ppm Hg2+/CQD suspension. Here the optical change was detected using a fluorescence spectrometer and observed with a digital camera.

The resulting CQDs were uniformly coated over polyvinylidene fluoride (PVdF) filter paper (Millipore, nominal pore size: 220 nm; diameter: 47 mm). The CQD suspension (100 ppm) was poured and then deposited (gravity feed) onto the filtering paper, forming CQD-coated membrane. The membrane was placed into an oven and then heated at 80 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, Hg2+ solution (concentration: 10 ppm) was dropped over the CQD-coated membrane. A digital camera was used to photograph the CQD-coated membrane under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation (wavelength: 360 nm). An iodide solution (concentration: 10 ppm) was also dropped to the solution for regenerating the exhausted membrane; as a result, the iodide regeneration process was observed at ambient temperature using the digital camera.

Liquid-phase adsorption isotherm of mercury ions on CQDs was carried out at 30 °C. The Hg2+ solution with an initial concentration of 100 ppm was prepared for the adsorption experiment. The CQD served as the adsorbent and was added to the ionic solution. The adsorption process was performed in a water bath with a rotational speed of 120 rpm at constant temperature. After attaining adsorption equilibrium (1 h), the residual solution was separated from the CQD slurry using a filter. The Hg2+ concentration of the residual solution was measured by atomic absorption spectroscopy (Shimadzu, AA-7000). The adsorption capacity of mercury ions by CQDs was subsequently determined (based on the mass balance equation).

HR-TEM micrographs of as-prepared CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 . As illustrated in Fig. 1, a homogeneous dispersion of carbon particles was achieved through the SPMA pyrolysis of carbon precursors. The spherical CQDs are found to have a homogeneous dispersion with a narrow distribution, ranged from 2.5 to 5.5 nm. The selected-area electron diffraction from an area focused on a single CQD, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 1(a)‒(c), reveals the presence of bright diffraction spots along with diffraction rings. The circular rings associated with each CQD demonstrate a polycrystalline structures. Fig. 1(d)‒(f) illustrate that each spherical-like CQD particle possess superior crystallinity with a lattice distance of 0.21‒0.22 nm, corresponding to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene sheets [43]. Both CQD-1 and CQD-2 samples contain a layered crystal structure, whereas CQD-3 sample is composed of several triangular domains (i.e., graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)), resulting in a circular shape. The formation of spherical shape is due to thermodynamic equilibrium for minimizing edge free energies, through atomic migration of uncombined and rearrangement of atoms (e.g., C, N, O), and reconstruction of crystallization [44,45] during SPMA process.

XPS was performed to further examine the surface chemical composition and functional group distribution of CQD samples. The C, N and O atomic ratios of CQDs can be determined from the survey scan of XPS spectra, in which the peaks at 284.6, 399.6, and 531.5 eV correspond to C 1s peak of sp2 carbon, N 1s peak of the doped nitrogen and O 1s peak of the decorated oxygen, respectively [13]. The surface N/C ratios are 36, 39, and 158 at.% for CQD-1, CQD-2, and CQD-3 sample, respectively. The O/C atomic ratio was in the following order: CQD-1 (33 at.%) < CQD-2 (38 at.%) < CQD-3 (99 at.%). This observation reveals that the urea content improves the surface amidation level along with the surface oxidation extent on the resulting CQDs.

An ICP-OES/MS analysis was used to measure the atomic compositions of bulk materials. The same sequence of N/C atomic ratio is obtained by the ICP-OES/MS analysis. The N/C ratios of CQD-1, CQD-2, and CQD-3 samples were 29, 48, and 79 at.%, respectively. Apparently, the magnitude of N/C ratio determined by XPS is much higher than that by ICP-OES/MS, referring to the formation of N-rich skin layer on CQD samples. These O/C ratios, determined from ICP-OES/MS, are 60, 56, and 68 at.% for CQD-1, CQD-2, and CQD-3 samples, respectively, which are higher than the values evaluated using XPS. This finding indicates the existence of O-rich core in both CQD-1 and CQD-2 samples. However, the O/C ratios have a little difference by the urea content.

To inspect the distribution of oxygen functional group on CQD samples, the C 1s peaks are decomposed into five peaks at ca. 284.4 eV (CC or C‒C), 285.2 eV (C‒N), 287.5 eV (C‒OH), 288.5 eV (CO), and 289.5 eV (O‒CO), as shown in Fig. 2 (a)‒(c). It was observed that the distributions of oxygen functional groups among various samples are different. For sample, CQD-3 sample possesses the largest fraction of carboxylic group, as compared to other two samples. Fig. 2(d)‒(f) shows asymmetric N 1s peaks that are deconvoluted into three signals: pyrrolic and pyridinic N at 398.3‒399.6 eV, quaternary N at 400.6‒401.4 eV, and N-oxides (e.g., OC‒N) at 403.1 eV [12,13]. The first peak is mainly attributed to the presence of (sp2) C‒NC (sp2), in which nitrogen was bonded to two carbon atoms or pyridine moiety, the second one is assigned to quaternary N which is bonded to three sp2 carbon atoms, known as “graphitic nitrogen” [46,47], and the last one corresponds to amide‒carbonyl group (OC‒NH2), e.g., amino and amide carbonyl functionalization at edges of graphene sheets [48].

We observe that both pyrrolic/pyridinic and quaternary N sites are predominant in the N-group distribution. This reflects that the thermal carbonization of carbon precursor (i.e., citric acid + urea) via SPMA method generates thermally-stable heterocyclic aromatic moieties such as pyridine, pyrrole, and quaternary type N sites (‘‘lattice N’’) [49]. Accordingly, both CQD-2 and CQD-3 samples contain a large number of lattice N sites, favoring charge transfer in sp2 domain and g-C3N4 grains, respectively. This observation was also confirmed by HR-TEM analysis. It is worth noting that only CQD-1 sample contain amide‒carbonyl group attached to the edge sites of GQD nanoparticles. Among these samples, CQD-1 sample possesses the highest carbon content decorating multiple O and N functional groups, including carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, phenolic, and amide‒carbonyl groups, based on the XPS and ICP-OES/MS analyses.

The optical images of different CQD suspensions in distilled water under UV irradiation (365 nm) are collected, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). Under UV light, CQD suspensions emit deep blue (CQD-1), green (CQD-2), and yellow-green (CQD-3) colors. This observation implies that high N/C ratio (i.e., from high urea content in the mixture of citric acid + urea) on CQD sample induces a red-shift in PL spectra. Fig. 3(b)‒(d) shows PL emission spectra of different CQD suspensions in distilled water at 360 nm. The PL responses of CQD-1, CQD-2, and CQD-3 samples demonstrate two major bands at ca. 450, 500 and 520 nm, respectively. The maximal wavelengths are usually attributed to the corresponding colors within the visible light range: from blue to green light, which are in good agreement with the observations from the optical photograph (see Fig. 3(a)). The colors gradually become darker with an increase in Hg2+ concentration due to the influence of fluorescence quenching.

One series of experiments for detecting mercury ions by CQD samples at different pH values (=4, 6, and 8) was done, as shown in the Electronic Supporting Information (see Fig. S1). We observe that the influence of pH value on the PL quenching of CQD-1 sample is significant, whereas the effect of pH on the detection performance of the other two samples (i.e., CQD-2 and CQD-3) seems to be minor. Within the pH range between 4 and 8, the deviations of PL intensity on GQD-1, GQD-2, and GQD-3 samples are 10.1, 2.2, and 3.1%, respectively. This major difference among all the samples can be attributed to surface charge density on various CQD samples at different pH values. Herein CQD-1 sample was found to possess the highest carbon content decorating multiple O and N functional groups, including carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, phenolic, and amide‒carbonyl groups. When the pH is higher than the point of zero charge, the CQD surface is negatively charged (attracting cations/repelling anions), i.e., favorable adsorption toward mercury ions. This can be supported by the argument that both O and N functionalities on CQD-1 sample can serve as active sites for adsorbing mercury ions.

To determine the sensitivity toward Hg2+ detection, the fluorescence quenching from GQDs was characterized via Stern–Volmer analysis, which correlates the PL intensity ratio to Hg2+ concentration [50,51]:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
[ C ]
where F 0 and F represents the PL intensities in the absence and the presence of Hg2+ ions, respectively. Herein, K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [C] is the concentration of the quencher. It is generally recognized that K SV equals to the product of k q and τ 0 (i.e., K SV = k q·τ 0) where k q represents the reaction rate constant and τ 0 is the lifetime of emissive excited state without the presence of quencher, respectively. The K SV values can be obtained from the slope of the linear Stern–Volmer plots of F 0/F versus [C], as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The correlation factor can be used to evaluate the linearity of Stern–Volmer plots, i.e., the detection limit. Accordingly, the fluorescence quenching on CQD-1 sample is linearly dependent on the ionic concentration within two regions: (i) 10‒100 and (ii) 500‒5000 ppb, as shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c), respectively. It is apparent that the fluorescence quenching initially occurs at an ultralow Hg2+ concentration (10 ppb). However, the other two samples exhibit a linear Stern–Volmer plots starting from 500 ppb (see Fig. 4(d)). Table 1 summaries one comparison regarding the detection limit toward Hg2+ ions by different CQD samples prepared from various synthesis methods [52–64]. As depicted in this table, CQD-1 suspension serves as a highly-sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting mercury ions at ppb-level in the aqueous solutions. However, to obtain the maximal adsorption capacity and the highest sensitivity, an in-depth investigation is required to figure out an optimal detection environment such as pH value, chemical composition, and solid content in batch system. The NCQDs can also be used as fluorescent probes for cell imaging, which have potential applications in bio-imaging and related fields [65]. Moreover, the K SV values are found to have the following order: CQD-1 (0.125 L/mg) > CQD-3 (0.068 L/mg) > CQD-2 (0.061 L/mg). This enhanced K SV value can be attributed to the fact that CQD-1 sample exhibits diverse functionalities and serves as an active site for adsorption of mercury ions. Therefore, it results in high sensitivity toward Hg2+ ions.

Notably, the CQD‒(Hg2+) x complex tend to aggregate and finally precipitate when the Hg2+ ionic are continuously added to the solution containing electroactive species with a constant concentration (i.e., 10 ppm). Fig. 5 (a) confirms the sediment of Hg species on the bottom of CQD suspensions where CQD-1 suspension exhibits a larger amount of grey precipitates, as compared to the other two samples. This can attributed to the fact that CQD-1 sample contains the highest carbon content decorating O and N functional groups, serving as active sites for specific adsorption of mercury ions. To elaborate the mechanism of Hg sediments formation, the FE-SEM spectra of the sediments on CQD-1 suspension at low and high magnifications were recorded (Fig. 5(b) and (c), respectively). As illustrated in Fig. 5, several Hg-based spheres partially covered by CQDs can be identified. Each Hg-based sphere has an average diameter of 10 µm. An energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, associated with the FE-SEM confirms the presence of HgO and Hg metals. Therefore, this technique provides a robust and reliable methodology to detect and collect toxic Hg2+ ions that are present in aqueous solution using CQDs. It can also be inferred that as-prepared CQDs serve as sensing probe as well as high-performance adsorbent in this process.

To further elaborate on the sensing mechanism, one possible reaction concerning the detection and collection of mercury ions using CQDs was proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . The reaction mechanism consists of the following steps: (a) fluorescence emitting under UV irradiation, (b) fluorescence quenching due to the formation of CQD‒(Hg2+) x complex, and (c) sedimentation of Hg-based spheres when reaching adsorption saturation. The inset of Fig. 6 depicts three possible pathways for forming CQD‒(Hg2+) x complex. In path (i), the surface reaction regarding the formation of Hg-intermediates on oxidized carbon surface can be formulated as follows [40,66]:
(R1)
2
C x
O
H
+
+ H
g
2 +
→
(
C x
O
)
2
H
g
2 +
+ 2
H
+

In path (ii), the adsorptive sites for adsorbing Hg2+ ions are formed due to the presence of amide-carbonyl groups (OC‒NH2), e.g., amino- and amid-carbonyl functionalization at the edges of CQDs. The third pathway (i.e., path (iii)) can be ascribed to strong interaction (i.e., dipole moment) between hydrophilic carbon surface (e.g., phenolic group) and mercury hydration molecules, where a single Hg2+ ion tends to bound with seven water molecules, forming [Hg(H2O)7]2+ complexes [67].

The formation of surface intermediates, i.e., GQD‒(Hg2+) x complex, effectively hinders the photon rejection from the highest occupied molecular orbital‒lowest unoccupied molecular orbital gap, inducing a fluorescence quenching phenomenon. When the adsorption saturation is reached (i.e., at high surface coverage), a lateral interaction between Hg-intermediates becomes more evident due to strong surface tension of the mercury ions (i.e., surface tension: 476 mN/m at 20 °C). The lateral interaction, generally stronger than the interfacial interaction between CQDs and Hg2+ ions, imparts to merge Hg clusters, thus forming microscaled Hg-based spheres. The Hg-based spheres are prone to precipitation; thus, the purification of the drinking water within adsorption units can be accomplished via collecting toxic Hg ions that precipitate out of the solution in the form of spherical sediments.

To explore the applicability of this approach for industrial scales, the CQD-coated PVdF membranes were employed to analyze the fluorescence quenching under UV irradiation at different configurations. Fig. 7 (a) demonstrates one set of photographs for three CQD-coated membranes under visible light illumination. With no Hg2+ ions within the solution (10 ppm), the particles emit blue, green, and yellow-green colors under the UV irradiation (360 nm), as shown in Fig. 7(b). As illustrated, the spectra tend to become darker upon dropping the Hg2+ ions into the solution, i.e., fluorescence quenching (see Fig. 7(c)). As shown in Fig. 7(d), their colors almost change to original ones in the presence of iodide anions. This fluorescence enhancement indicates that the iodide can strip and refresh Hg-contaminated sites, leading to the regeneration of CQD surfaces. Therefore, a selective regeneration toward GQD‒(Hg2+) x complex can be accomplished using the iodide anions bound to the adsorbed Hg2+ ions. The iodide anions form HgI2 and thereby regenerate the free CQDs [68].

It is also important to investigate the selectivity of as-synthesized CQDs toward different metallic ions that can be found within the water. To this end, various metallic ions (with a similar concentration among different samples) were added to an aqueous solution and the change of fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of these ions to the solution was recorded. Figs. S2‒S4 highlight the selectivity as well as the PL intensities of CQD suspensions contaminated with various metallic ions. The quenching of fluorescence response on CQDs exposed to various metallic ions clearly shows superb selectivity toward Hg2+ ions since there are no significant spectral changes of CQDs in the presence of Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Cr3+ ions. However, in the case of Fe3+, a slight spectral change was observed. Moreover, all CQD suspensions display very stable PL intensity upon adding (i) 10 ppm Cd2+ followed by 10 ppm Zn2+ and (ii) 10 ppm Cu2+ followed by 10 ppm Ni2+ into 1 ppm Hg2+/CQD suspension. In each case, the maximal deviations for CQD-1, CQD-2, and CQD-3 samples were 0.1, 4.8, and 2.1%, respectively. This reveals that CQD-1 sample delivers the highest stability and selectivity toward Hg2+ ions among all the samples.

Adsorption isotherm of mercury ions on GQD-1 sample at 30 °C is provided in Fig. 8 . A couple of two-parameter isotherm equations, Langmuir and Freundlich, are applied for interpretation of the adsorption data obtained during the experiments outlined in the preceding section. Correlating the equilibrium data to the adsorption equation is essential for understanding the liquid-phase adsorption in aqueous solutions. Such an analysis also enables identifying the key mechanistic steps within the adsorption process. Employing the Freundlich equation one can write [69]:
(2)
Q e
=
K F
C e
1 / n

Here, K F and n are the Freundlich constants used to model the adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity, respectively. One crucial quantitative metric for assessing the favorability of the adsorption process is the magnitude of 1/n (for a favorable adsorption 1/n is smaller than unity). The Freundlich isotherm parameters for the adsorption of Hg2+ onto CQDs are provided in Table 2 (based on a linear fit to Eq. (2)). According to Table 2, the Hg2+ removal is a favorable adsorption process since the 1/n value is smaller than unity.

The other model, Langmuir equation, is formulated assuming a monolayer adsorption of Hg2+ ions onto GQDs [69].
(3)
Q e
=
Q max
K L
C e
/
(
1 +
K L
C e
)
where C e is the concentration of Hg2+ ions at equilibrium, q e represents the amount of Hg2+ ions per unit mass of CQDs, Q L resembles the amount of Hg2+ ions adsorbed per unit mass of CQDs assuming a complete monolayer coverage, and K L is the Langmuir constant, a key parameter for assessing free energy of adsorption [69,70]. Fitting a linear trendline to Eq. (3) ((C e/Q e) versus C e) the values of Q L and K L can be deduced from the slope and intercept of the linear plot. Table 2 provides a summary of the Langmuir parameters calculated for the adsorption of Hg2+ onto CQDs. Therefore, considering the correlation coefficients, the Langmuir model (r 2: 0.999) provides a better fit compared to the Freundlish formulation (r 2: 0.858) as illustrated in Fig. 8. Interestingly, the q m value of CQD-1 sample can attain as high as 3.33 g/g, much higher than various carbon adsorbents (e.g., active carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes) [40–42]. The removal efficiency of Hg2+ ions on CQDs is ca. 1280, 6, and 16 times higher than that on active carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes, respectively. Therefore, based on the analysis provided in this work, CQDs can be considered as highly-sensitive and -selective detection probing materials in addition to an efficient adsorbent toward Hg2+ ions that are present in aqueous solutions.

We have successfully synthesized CQDs through the SPMA method of citric acid and urea at 250 °C. The fluorescence quenching ratio in CQD suspensions was proportional to Hg2+ concentration analyzed by Stern-Volmer model. The detection limit of CQDs toward Hg2+ ions can reach as high as 10 ppb, which meet the standard requirements of drinking water. Analyzed by Langmuir model, the maximal adsorption capacity of Hg2+ onto CQDs could attain as high as 3.33 g/g, which is ca. 1280, 6, and 16 times higher than the values reported for active carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes, respectively. Notably, Hg-based spheres with an average diameter of 10 µm are formed at adsorption equilibrium due to lateral interaction among CQD‒(Hg2+) x complexes. The CQD-coated polymeric membrane also exhibited the fluorescence quenching and it was efficiently refreshed by iodide anions for consecutive usage. Therefore, the functionalized CQDs synthesized in this work serve as highly-selective/-sensitive sensing probes as well as superior adsorbent toward Hg2+ ions that exist in aqueous solutions. Thus, we believe that the CQD-based films can be used in larger scales for adsorption and recovery of Hg2+ contaminants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful for the support from the Ministry of Science and Technology (Taiwan, R.O.C.) under the contracts MOST 105-2628-E-155-002-MY3 and MOST 105-2221-E-155-014-MY3. Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2E0082 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation, Taiwan) is deeply appreciated.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2019.04.012.
